one_line_takeaway: A closed-form, theory-derived checkerboard luminance trigger achieves near-perfect clean-label backdoor success at extremely low poisoning budgets (e.g., 20 samples / 0.04% on CIFAR-10) with no surrogate-model training, and evades 9 representative defenses plus 3 defenses the authors specifically adapted against it.
key_finding: On CIFAR-10, poisoning only 20 samples (0.04% poisoning rate) at a 10/255 training-time budget yields 95.72% ASR at ×1 amplification and 99.99% ASR at ×2, versus the strongest matched baseline (BAAT) at 34.99% and most other baselines below 1% ASR, while clean accuracy stays at 94.55% (vs. 94.14% clean-model baseline). On CIFAR-100, Checkerboard reaches 96.39% ASR at ×1 (99.84% at ×2) versus Narcissus's 33.55% at ×2. On CelebA, Checkerboard achieves 100% ASR at ×1. On ImageNet-100 with CSS enabled, poisoning 600 samples (0.46% rate) gives 57.33% ASR at ×1 rising to 94.17% at ×2, without degrading clean accuracy (69.82% vs. 70.34% clean baseline); random sampling without CSS on ImageNet-100 collapses to 0.08% ASR, confirming CSS is necessary (not merely helpful) on texture-rich data. Poison-generation runtime is under 0.1s on CIFAR-10/100/CelebA and ~2.15s on ImageNet-100 (dominated by CSS), versus learning-based baselines (COMBAT, GenBound, LC) exceeding 10^5 seconds on ImageNet-100. Ablation on trigger pattern confirms the derived checkerboard (95.72% ASR) beats vertical stripes (84.01%), horizontal stripes (58.44%), salt-and-pepper (4.15%), and random noise (0.64%), supporting the theoretical derivation empirically. Against defenses: BTI-DBF's synthesized triggers produce a near-uniform (~10% per class) prediction distribution rather than isolating the trigger; MM-BD's target-class maximum-margin (5.03) falls within the normal range (4.73-5.96) with anomaly score 0.25; ABL drops clean accuracy to 91.37% while ASR stays at 97.63%, and the paper shows poisoned-sample losses do not form the expected early low-loss group, violating ABL's core assumption; FT-SAM retains 94.54% ACC with ASR still 94.81%; Beatrix's RMMD statistic stays near zero; PoisonSpot achieves only 5% TPR at 73.84% FPR; FREAK gets 49.05% TPR/13.86% FPR; REBACK's transfer ratio stays near 1.0 (below its >5.0 threshold) so it reports no backdoor; FAD's TPR is only 3.14%. For adaptive defenses: Checkerboard-Notch (trigger-aware subtraction) reduces ×1 ASR to 50.04% but amplification restores 89.19% (×2) and 99.78% (×3); default pixel-wise trigger is defeated by Gaussian blur σ≥1.0 (ASR drops near 0), but a coarser block-size-2 variant (with poisoning budget raised from 20 to 100 samples) recovers 70.84% ASR at σ=1.0 and 51.31% at σ=2.0 (99.84%/98.42% with ×2 amplification); the class-wise CGE outlier detector fails to flag the true target class (anomaly scores 0.054 on CIFAR-10 and 0.060 on ImageNet-100, both below the max non-target scores of 0.079 and 0.148).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is Checkerboard, a learning-free clean-label backdoor attack whose trigger is derived analytically rather than learned. The authors formulate trigger design as maximizing linear separability (Fisher Discriminant Ratio) between poisoned and clean samples under an additive-trigger model p = x + αδ. Solving this in closed form (Eq. 1-3), restricting the trigger to a luminance template replicated across RGB channels to align with early-layer edge-detector inductive biases (Eq. 4), and then approximating the pixel covariance with a local Gaussian Markov Random Field on a 4-connected image lattice (assumptions A1/A2) reduces the continuous optimization to a discrete problem (Eq. 5) whose provably optimal solution on the bipartite pixel grid is a checkerboard pattern of alternating +1/-1 luminance values. The paper also contributes Complexity-driven Sample Selection (CSS), which ranks target-class images by a Sobel-gradient-based Convolutional Gradient Energy (CGE) score (Eq. 6-7) and poisons the smoothest (lowest-CGE) samples to preserve trigger-to-background contrast on texture-rich, high-resolution datasets.
threat_model: Data-outsourcing / training-set-poisoning scenario: the victim aggregates training data from multiple external sources; the attacker controls a subset of that data but cannot alter the training procedure or model architecture. Attacker goals: backdoor activation on trigger-carrying inputs, preservation of clean-task accuracy, and stealthiness (clean-label consistency plus an ℓ∞-bounded perturbation). Attacker capabilities are deliberately restricted to 'near-zero-knowledge trigger generation' (trigger derived analytically with no access to the victim's training set or a surrogate model, only the prior that the task is natural-image classification) and 'restricted data poisoning' (access only to the target-class subset D_t, and can poison at most p_num samples while preserving original labels); the adversary does not know the victim's model architecture.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitation: the default pixel-wise checkerboard trigger is sensitive to strong low-pass preprocessing (Gaussian blur, and per the text, also mean filtering/JPEG/DCT suppression addressed only in an appendix not extracted here) because of its inherent high-frequency structure; the authors present this as an attacker-side tradeoff resolvable by using a coarser (lower-frequency) block pattern at the cost of a higher poisoning budget (20→100 samples on CIFAR-10), rather than a fundamental break. Other constraints evident from the paper's own design, not stated as limitations per se: the closed-form derivation relies on approximating the true pixel covariance by a local GMRF under natural-image-statistics assumptions (A1/A2), which is an approximation rather than an exact match to real data covariance; CSS requires access to the full target-class subset to rank by CGE; defense evaluation for the 9 detailed defenses plus adaptive defenses is conducted primarily on CIFAR-10 (with the CGE adaptive detector also tested on ImageNet-100), so generalization of the defense-evasion results to CIFAR-100/CelebA is not directly demonstrated in the paper; effectiveness scales with victim model capacity (weaker/harder-to-train architectures like ViT-Small show markedly lower baseline ASR of 53.58% at ×1, needing ×2 amplification to reach 94.58%).

quantitative_results: CIFAR-10: Checkerboard ×1 ASR 95.72%, ×2 ASR 99.99%, ACC 94.55% (clean baseline ACC 94.14%)
CIFAR-100: Checkerboard ×1 ASR 96.39%, ×2 ASR 99.84%, ACC 76.66% (clean baseline ACC 76.78%)
CelebA: Checkerboard ×1 ASR 100%, ACC 79.24% (clean baseline ACC 79.49%)
ImageNet-100 (with CSS): Checkerboard ×1 ASR 57.33%, ×2 ASR 94.17%, ACC 69.82% (clean baseline ACC 70.34%)
ImageNet-100 without CSS (random selection): ASR collapses to 0.08%
Poison-generation runtime: <0.1s on CIFAR-10/100/CelebA, ~2.15s on ImageNet-100 (mostly CSS); learning-based baselines like COMBAT/GenBound/LC exceed 10^5 s on ImageNet-100
Trigger-pattern ablation (CIFAR-10, ×1 ASR): Checkerboard 95.72%, vertical stripes 84.01%, horizontal stripes 58.44%, salt-and-pepper 4.15%, random noise 0.64%

Architecture ablation (CIFAR-10, ×1 ASR/ACC): DenseNet121 98.83%/95.53%, ResNet18 95.72%/94.55%, VGG16 92.13%/93.06%, MobileNetV3 89.09%/92.67%, CCT 76.06%/88.38%, ViT-Small 53.58%/83.72% (rising to 94.58% ASR at ×2)
Extreme low-budget: 1 poisoned sample at 10/255 with ×2 test-time amplification (20/255) yields 99.15% ASR on CIFAR-10

Defense results (CIFAR-10 unless noted): BTI-DBF recovers a ~uniform (~10%/class) prediction distribution (fails); MM-BD target-class max-margin 5.03 within normal range 4.73-5.96, anomaly score 0.25 (fails); ABL drops ACC to 91.37% while ASR stays 97.63% (fails); FT-SAM ACC 94.54% / ASR 94.81% (fails); Beatrix RMMD near zero (fails); PoisonSpot TPR 5% / FPR 73.84% (fails); FREAK TPR 49.05% / FPR 13.86% (largely fails); REBACK transfer ratio ~1.0 vs >5.0 threshold, reports no backdoor (fails); FAD TPR 3.14% (fails)

Adaptive defenses: Checkerboard-Notch reduces ×1 ASR to 50.04% (ACC 94.72%) but ×2/×3 amplification restores 89.19%/99.78%; Gaussian blur σ=1.0/2.0 defeats the default block-size-1 trigger (ASR ~0.67%/0.85-1.04%) but a block-size-2 trigger with poisoning raised to 100 samples recovers 70.84% ASR at σ=1.0 (99.84% at ×2) and 51.31% at σ=2.0 (98.42% at ×2); class-wise CGE detector anomaly scores for the true target class are 0.054 (CIFAR-10) and 0.060 (ImageNet-100), below the largest non-target scores of 0.079 and 0.148 (fails to flag the target class)
